head.ce:title: Carbon dots with tailor-made chelating ligands for specific metal ions recognition: Target synthesis and prediction of metal ions selectivity

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Carbon dots with different chelating ligands were target synthesized via free radical polymerization in combination of hydrothermal carbonization technology, in which precursors were directional converted into carbon dots. The chelate functionalized carbon dots modified with EDTA and HEDP can achieve specific recognition of Fe3+ and Cu2+ and enhanced magnetic resonance imaging.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: The continuous development of industry and manufacturing makes heavy metal pollution a worldwide problem [1,2]. Wastewater containing heavy metal ions is released into water environment and spread through the food chain, posing a serious threat to human life [3]. In particular, excess iron ions can lead to various types of cancer and degrade the function of organs such as the heart, lungs and pancreas [4,5]. Excess Cu2+ ingestion can result in headaches, dizziness, Wilson's and Menkes diseases [6,7]. Whereas, copper and iron ions are involved in many basic physiological processes, such as cell metabolism, enzymatic reaction and maintenance of various organs [8,9]. Consequently, based on the physiological and pathological importance of metal ions, it will be of great significance to develop a method to achieve specific recognition of metal ions in water [10,11]. Among the various analysis methods, fluorescence spectroscopy has grown in popularity due to its high sensitivity, simple operation, low cost and good reproducibility [12]. Carbon dots (CDs) have served as fluorescent probes to recognize heavy metal ions [13–16]. Generally, CDs are less than 10 nm in size and usually composed of carbon core and abundant functional groups on the surface [17–19]. Hydrothermal carbonization of precursor is often used to synthesize CDs, however the synthetic yield of CDs is usually low [20,21].

CDs have attracted great attention as fluorescent probes due to the advantages of water dispersibility, high fluorescence intensity and tunable surface functionality. However, the abundance of functional groups (i.e., hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups) on the surface of CDs result in their simultaneous response to multiple ions and poor selectivity [22,23]. For instance, the functional groups –NH2 and –OH on CDs surface made CDs exhibit fluorescence response to Cu2+ and Fe3+, respectively [22]. The functional groups such as –COOH and –NH2 anchored on the surface of CDs showed specific recognition for Cu2+ [24]. Surface-different nitrogen-doped CDs derived from different ionic liquids presented disparate selectivity for Cu2+ and Fe3+ [25]. The NH2-CDs serve as multifunctional fluorescence nanosensors to detect various metal ions, such as Co2+, Cr3+ or even Fe3+ [26,27]. Moreover, Cu2+ was selectively detected due to rich surface groups of –COOH and –OH on CDs [28]. Despite this, the correlation between CDs with different functional groups and metal binding remains unclear, resulting in the inability to predict the specific recognition of metal ions. CDs modified with EDTA have higher selectivity and more sensitivity than CDs for metal ion detection directly [29]. Wang et al. reported CDs modified with EDTA exhibited higher sensitivity for Cr (VI) detection with a LOD of 10 nM compared with CDs [30]. The fluorescent probe in sodium dodecyl sulfate micelles with EDTA showed highly sensitive and selective turn on detection for methylmercury over Hg2+ [31]. Inspired by metal-chelating affinity of chelating ligands, we hypothesize CDs modified with different chelating ligands can enhance sensitive and selective detection for metal ions through efficient chelation in predetermined spatial configuration [32,33]. Further, the chelating ligands have different coordination constants for different species of metal ions. The larger the coordination constants are, the stronger the metal-chelating affinity is, thus achieving specific recognition of metal ions [34,35].

Herein, CDs with tailor-made chelating ligands were target synthesized and allowed the specific recognition for Fe3+ and Cu2+, respectively. Primarily, CDs with tailor-made chelating ligands were target synthesized via free radical polymerization in combination of hydrothermal carbonization technology. Specifically, the chelate functionalized CDs, including EfCDs and HfCDs, were synthesized using chelating ligands EDTA and HEDP as precursors with high synthetic yields of 86.7% and 72.5%, respectively. Based on the strong metal-chelating affinity of EDTA and HEDP for Fe3+ and Cu2+ (lgKEDTA-Fe = 25.1, lgKHEDP -Cu = 12.5), we predicted and confirmed that EfCDs and HfCDs specifically recognize Fe3+ and Cu2+, respectively. The exponential type functions between coordination constant (lgK) of EDTA or HEDP and fluorescence quenching degree (1-F/F
0 ) have been established, which promote the prediction of metal ion specific recognition. In brief, CDs with different tailor-made chelating ligands system could potentially facilitate the specific recognition of metal ions, which is of great significance for water environment monitoring and biomedical science.

Acrylamide (AM), EDTA were purchased from Tianjin Guangfu Chemical Research Institute (Tianjin, China). HEDP was purchased from Harbin Yongchang reagent co., LTD. Ammonium persulfate (APS), FeCl3·6H2O, CuSO4·5H2O, MnCl2·H2O, CoCl2·6H2O, AgNO3, Pb(NO3)2 and Hg(NO3)2·2H2O were purchased from Fuchen Chemical Reagent Co. (Tianjin, China). Absolute ethanol was purchased from Zhiyuan Chemical Reagent Co. (Tianjin, China). All solutions were prepared using deionized water from a Milli-Q water purification system with resistivity of 18.2 MΩ. Dialysis bags (MWCO = 500 Da) were obtained from Solarbio (USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Target synthesis of CDs with EDTA and HEDP chelating ligands via free radical polymerization in combination of hydrothermal carbonization process
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, 28 mmol AM, 1 mmol EDTA, 0.04 mmol APS were dissolved in Milli-Q water (30 mL). After ultrasonic, the mixture was transferred into Teflon-lined autoclaves at 220 °C for 12 h. Subsequently, the autoclaves cooled down to room temperature. And then, the obtained solution were centrifuged at 7000 rpm for 10 min and filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane to remove large carbonized particles. Then a dialysis process was performed for 6 h in 50% ethanol-50% deionized water, followed by another 6 h in deionized water using a dialysis bag (MWCO = 500 Da). Lastly, the products were freeze-dried to obtain the EDTA functionalized CDs (EfCDs). In the same way, replaced EDTA with HEDP and obtained HEDP functionalized CDs (HfCDs).

Synthetic yield of CDs is a significant evaluation index for the conversion efficiency of precursors. The synthetic yield of CDs was calculated according to equation (1) [36]:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Qualitative characterizations of chelate functional groups of chelate functionalized CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopies (1H NMR) were recorded by NEXUS-870 spectrometer with D2O as solvent to characterize the chemical structure of chelate functional units. The functional groups on the surface of CDs were confirmed using FTIR (Nicolet 380, Thermo Electron Corporation, MA, USA). The surface properties of CDs were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray) using Al/Kα as the exciting source.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative characterization of metal ion recognition ability of chelate functionalized CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The coordination constant (K) and chelation sites (n) of the metal ions-chelate functionalized CDs complexes were calculated by the Benesi-Hildebrand equation [37,38] and double logarithmic chelation equation [39], respectively. The chelating ability of the chelate functionalized CDs was quantitatively measured:

ce:section.ce:section-title: The specific recognition of chelate functionalized CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the selectivity of chelate functionalized CDs to metal ions, photolumiescence (PL) intensity quenching experiments were performed. Using chelate functionalized CDs as probes to detect different metal ions such as Ca2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Sn4+, Nd3+, Ag+ and Fe3+ at the same concentration (300 μM). The fluorescence intensity was measured at the optimum excitation wavelength. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of EfCDs and HfCDs with different concentration of Fe3+ and Cu2+ (0–500 μM) were investigated, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative relationship between the fluorescence quenching degree and coordination constant
ce:section.ce:para: Further, in the Support Information, the following are provided in detail: Other sample preparation methods (S1), characterization methods and anti-interference ability of CDs (S2), relaxivity measurement and MRI in vitro (S3) and CCK8 assay evaluate the biocompatibility (S4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Target synthesis of chelate functionalized CDs via free radical polymerization in combination of hydrothermal carbonization process
ce:section.ce:para: Some problems such as low synthetic yield, ambiguous correlation between CDs modified with different functional groups and metal ions, as well as unpredictable recognizable metal ions have restricted the development of CDs. To solve these problems, a method of free radical polymerization in combination of hydrothermal carbonization was proposed to obtain CDs with tailor-made chelating ligands. As displayed in Fig. 1 a, the monomer acts as a fluorescence precursor, introducing chelating ligands, through monomer free radical polymerization, chain transfer to chelating ligands, PAM chain crosslinking as well as hydrothermal carbonization, chelate functionalized CDs are obtained. In the process, the directional transformation of precursors has been realized, so the synthetic yield of CDs is improved. In addition, chelate functionalized CDs inherit the strong metal-chelating affinity of chelating ligands, thus achieving the prediction of metal ions recognition.

According to equation (1), the calculated synthetic yields are shown in Fig. 1b. The synthetic yields of HfCDs without APS and EfCDs without APS were 7.8% and 14.7%, and the synthetic yields of HfCDs without AM and EfCDs without AM were 10.6% and 28.9%. Compared with the low synthetic yields, the synthetic yields of HfCDs and EfCDs were significantly increased to 72.5% and 86.7%. These results can be attributed to the fact that initiator can act on small molecule precursors containing CC bonds to promote free radical polymerization. By opening CC with addition polymerization, the precursors were directional transformed, and the problem of low synthetic yields caused by side reaction was avoided. If the precursors do not contain AM, it is difficult to generate polymerization reaction since EDTA and HEDP do not contain CC bonds, thereby reducing the synthetic yields of CDs.

Furthermore, monomer concentration is a key factor affecting the degree of polymerization, the degree of polymerization determines the synthetic yields. Therefore, the effect of polymerization degree on the synthetic yield of CDs was investigated by changing the AM concentration [40–42]. The synthetic yields of CDs prepared with different AM concentration are shown in Fig. 1c. Distinctly, with the increase of AM concentration (from 0 to 0.93 mmol/mL), the synthetic yields also increased (HfCDs: from 10.6% to 72.5%, EfCDs: from 28.9% to 86.7%). The synthetic yields of HfCDs and EfCDs were increased by about 6 and 2 times, respectively. This is because with the increase of AM concentration, the non-conjugated polymer clusters increases. In the carbonization process, the non-conjugated polymer clusters were in a crosslinking state, which immobilized the converted intermediate, avoiding the loss of raw material.

Specifically, an overview of free radical polymerization in combination of hydrothermal carbonization is demonstrated in three stages to elaborate the reaction mechanism (Scheme 1). In the first stage (Scheme 1a), initiator broke down to free radical, which promoted AM monomers to polymerize forming polyacrylamide (PAM) in the hydrothermal condition [43]. In the process of PAM chain transfer reaction, PAM chains transfer to EDTA or HEDP in the solvent, PAM and chelating ligands were linked by covalent bonds to form PAM-ligand [44,45]. Then, PAM-ligand was dehydrated and crosslinked to form PAM-ligand clusters (Scheme 1b) [46]. This is similar to the process of AM forming clusters through dehydration and crosslinking reported [40]. Subsequently, the generated PAM-ligand clusters underwent hydrothermal carbonization (Scheme 1c). Finally, the chelate functionalized CDs were obtained, which had carbon core and the chelate functional units. XRD results (Fig. S1) confirmed the existence of carbon core.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Qualitative and quantitative characterization of chelating ligands of chelate functionalized CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of HfCDs was revealed by TEM. As shown in Fig. 2 a, HfCDs were evenly dispersed, with relatively uniform particle size and without agglomeration. According to the statistical measurement results of 200 particles from TEM, the particle size of HfCDs was evenly distributed between 2 and 4 nm, with an average particle size of 3 nm.

Furthermore, the NMR spectra were used to investigate the chemical structure of EfCDs and HfCDs. The 1H NMR spectrum of NfCDs (Fig. 2b) showed wide overlapping signals in the range of 1–3 ppm related to PAM protons (Fig. 2e). Further, the 1H NMR spectrum of EfCDs containing two signals in the range of 1–3 ppm were similar to the former, but the signals corresponding to III, IV and II ambient hydrogen in Fig. 2c appeared at 2.6, 3.5 and 3.6 ppm, indicating the existence of EDTA structural units [47,48]. Besides, the signals of I and II in the range of 1–3 ppm were the same as NfCDs. While the signals at 1.2 and 3.4 ppm corresponded to III and II ambient hydrogen in Fig. 2d, indicating the existence of HEDP structural units [49–51]. Therefore, the above results can indicate that EfCDs and HfCDs were composed of the carbon core of carbonized PAM and the chelating ligand functional units on the surface.

FTIR and XPS spectra were performed to trace the functional groups on the surface of EfCDs and HfCDs. Fig. 2f presented the FTIR spectrum of EfCDs. The bands at 3203, 2942 and 1562 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of N–H, –CH2 and –CO–NH- in PAM, respectively [52–54]. The absorption bands at 3457, 1658, 1455 and 1377 cm−1 were associated with O–H, CO, C–H and C–N from EDTA, respectively [22,55,56]. Moreover, XPS results (Fig. S2) confirmed NfCDs, EfCDs and HfCDs mainly contain C, N and O three elements. As shown in Fig. 2g, the high resolution C 1s spectra of EfCDs were fitted into three characteristic peaks at 284.8, 285.6, and 288.9 eV, attributing to C–C, C–O/C–N, and CO, respectively [10,57]. The N 1s XPS spectra (Fig. 2h) displayed two N states of N–C at 399.7 eV and N–H at 401.3 eV [10]. The FTIR spectrum of HfCDs is displayed in Fig. 2i. The peaks around 3392, 2168 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibration of O–H and PO, respectively [58]. The characteristic absorption peak of P–O stretching vibration appears at 1112 and 848 cm−1 derived from HEDP [59,60]. The stretching modes of –NH (3216 cm−1), –CH2 (2930 cm−1) and –CO–NH- (1553 cm−1) were ascribed to PAM [54]. The high-resolution C 1s XPS spectra of HfCDs (Fig. 2j) contained three peaks at 284.5, 286.9 and 288.3 eV, which can be ascribed to C–C, C–P/C–O/C–N, and CO bands, respectively [10,24]. The high-resolution P 2p XPS spectra (Fig. 2k) exhibited three peaks of P–C (129.5 eV), P–O (132.6 eV) and PO (134.8 eV) [61–63]. In addition, the quantitative evaluation of the binding energy in Table S1 further proved the chelate functionalized precursor had successfully incorporated into EfCDs or HfCDs. The 1H NMR and FTIR spectra of EfCDs without AM and HfCDs without AM (Fig. S3) showed that EDTA and HEDP retained the structure units after hydrothermal carbonization. The above results indicated that AM, EDTA and HEDP precursors were hydrothermal carbonized to form CDs, which were composed of carbon core and abundant functional groups on the surface. The chelate functionalized CDs could exert the ability of EDTA and HEDP to recognize metal ions. The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of EfCDs and HfCDs are shown in Fig. S4.

EDTA and HEDP, as common metal ion chelating ligands, own strong chelating affinity to metal ions (lgK
EDTA-Fe = 25.1, lgK
HEDP-Cu = 12.5). As shown in Fig. 3 a and b, EDTA and HEDP can form stable octahedral structure and micellar polynuclear complexes with Fe3+ and Cu2+, respectively. Hence, the two chelating ligands served as chelating functional precursors in the synthesis system, predicting EfCDs and HfCDs inherit the strong chelating affinity to Fe3+ or Cu2+. Benesi-Hildebrand equation and double logarithmic chelation equation were used to quantitatively evaluate the metal-chelating affinity of EfCDs and HfCDs. The coordination constant and the number of chelation sites of EfCDs and HfCDs to Fe3+ and Cu2+ were calculated, respectively. Based on chelation properties, the chelation constant was calculated in the region of low metal ion concentration, the chelation sites were calculated in the region of high metal ion concentration. The functional relationship between 1/(F–F0) and [(Fe3+)]−1 is shown in Fig. 3c, the coordination constant K was (3.92 ± 0.03) × 104 M−1. In the region with high Fe3+ concentration, there was no linear relationship between 1/(F–F0) and [(Fe3+)]−1, so the double logarithm chelation formula was used to calculate the chelation site, which was 3.62 ± 0.18 (Fig. 3d). The coordination constant K of HfCDs for Cu2+ was (5.95 ± 0.04) × 105 M−1 (Fig. 3e). In the region with high Cu2+ ion concentration, the correlation between 1/(F–F0) and [(Fe3+)]−1 was still linear, indicating the chelation sites n was 1 (Fig. 3f) [64]. The coordination constants in this work are comparable to other literatures (Table S2), indicating EfCDs and HfCDs can form stable complexes with the corresponding metal ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specific recognition performance of chelate functionalized CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The recognition ability of chelate functionalized CDs for different metal ions was evaluated. As displayed in Fig. 4 a and b, both the fluorescence quenching degree of Fe3+ to EfCDs and Cu2+ to HfCDs are more than 50%, indicating EDTA and HEDP functionalization can endow EfCDs and HfCDs the metal-chelating affinity to selectively recognize Fe3+ and Cu2+, respectively. Additionally, the anti-interference performance of EfCDs and HfCDs were studied. As shown in Fig. S5a and b, the coexisting metal ions caused the fluorescence changes were not obvious, which indicated the chelate functionalized CDs have effective anti-interference ability.

In order to prove chelate functionalized CDs can achieve specific recognition of metal ions, the exponential type functions between lgK and 1-F/F
0 were established. As shown in Fig. 4c and d, the fitting functions were y = 7.042 × 10−6 × exp (x/2.169) + 0.004 and y = 8.219 × 10−4 × exp (x/2.078) + 0.195, respectively. With the increased of lgK, the value of 1-F/F
0 increased in an exponential type mode. When lgK EDTA-Fe = 25.1 or lgK
HEDP-Cu = 12.5, the degree of fluorescence attenuation of EfCDs and HfCDs reached 75% and 54%, respectively. However, many metal ions, especially Fe3+ and Cu2+, have no obvious fluorescence quenching on NfCDs, indicating NfCDs without chelating ligands modification have no metal ion recognition ability. The results demonstrate EfCDs and HfCDs inherit the strong metal-chelating affinity of EDTA and HEDP, which facilitates the prediction of recognizable metal ions. In some previous researches, CDs with different functional groups were used as fluorescence probes to detect metal ions, but the correlation between functional groups and recognizable metal ions was obscure. For example, CDs with –OH and –COOH can recognize Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+ and Pb2+ ions, CDs with different functional groups such as –NH2, –NH2 and –COOH, –OH and –COOH all can recognize Cu2+ (Fig. 4e and Table S3). Therefore, CDs modified with functional groups cannot predict the types of detectable metal ions in advance. Nevertheless, CDs modified with chelating ligands inherit the strong chelating affinity of EDTA or HEDP for Fe3+ or Cu2+, realizing the prediction of recognizable metal ions. The established exponential type functions provide a theoretical basis for the specific recognition of metal ions by chelate functionalized CDs.

The fluorescence intensity of EfCD and HfCDs solutions decreases with the increase of Fe3+ or Cu2+ concentration from 0 to 500 μM, respectively (Fig. 4f and g). Apparently, the 1-F/F0 values displayed a good linear relationship versus the concentration of Fe3+ or Cu2+ in the range of 0–50 μM (Fig. 4h and i). Additionally, the LOD of EfCDs for Fe3+ is 33.8 nM, which is better than most other literatures (Fig. 4j) [22,27,65–70]. The LOD of HfCDs for Cu2+ is 88.7 nM, which is comparable to the other literatures (Fig. 4k) [22,28,69,71–75]. At present, the method of functional groups modification is usually used to improve the metal ions detection ability of CDs, but the results show that the method cannot completely improve the sensitivity of CDs, the correlation between functional groups and LOD is also unclear. Interestingly, CDs modified with chelating ligands inherit the strong metal-chelating affinity of chelating ligands, so that the chelate functionalized CDs have more excellent metal ion detection ability than those modified by other functional groups such as –NH2, –OH and –COOH.

Generally, the lifetime measurement technique is used to investigate the fluorescence quenching mechanism of CDs induced by metal ions [76,77]. According to the different lifetime results, the fluorescence quenching mechanism can be divided into static and dynamic quenching [27]. Static quenching takes place in the process of the formation of complexes between fluorophore group and quencher, while dynamic quenching occurs in the collision process of fluorescence group excited state and quencher. In order to explore the fluorescence quenching mechanism of CDs by metal ions, the EfCDs/Fe3+ system was taken as an example to explore the fluorescence decay curves of EfCDs in the absence and presence of Fe3+. As shown in Fig. 5 a and b, the fluorescence lifetime of EfCDs is 5.71 ± 0.03 ns, the introduction of Fe3+ had a negligible influence on lifetime (5.69 ± 0.02 ns). It indicates that EfCDs and Fe3+ form non fluorescent complexes, belonging to static quenching.

Finally, the enhancement effect of EfCDs on magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) was analyzed, Fe3+-EfCDs complexes enhance MRI signals (Fig. S6). The cytotoxicity of EfCDs and HfCDs was evaluated (Fig. S7), revealing outstanding biocompatibility.

Jiaxin Dong: Data curation, Visualization. Baoqiang Li: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Jin Xiao: Investigation. Guanxiong Liu: Visualization. Vladimir Baulin: Formal analysis, Funding acquisition. Yujie Feng: Supervision. Dechang Jia: Supervision. Aslan Yu Tsivadze: Funding acquisition. Yu Zhou: Supervision.
